text: We present a case of discoid resection of invasive rectosigmoid endometriosis. The incidence of bowel endometriosis ranges from three to thirty-seven percent. Symptoms include painful bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, alternating constipation and diarrhea, intestinal cramping, nausea and or vomiting, abdominal pain, rectal pain, rectal bleeding, and possibly appendicitis. Ninety percent of the lesions involve the sigmoid or rectal colon.

Multidisciplinary laparoscopic treatment of extensive bowel endometriosis is standard of care, and indications for bowel resection include invasion of more than fifty percent of the bowel circumference, multiple nodules or nodules larger than three centimeters. Complications include anastomotic leakages, pelvic abscess, erecto-vaginal fistula, the recurrence rate after bowel resection, can be quite high, and cumulative pregnancy rates after bowel resection are acceptable.

The laparoscopic view shows the pelvis with the bladder cuff and sigmoid colon visible. The area of invasive endometriosis is highlighted in a circled region, with other areas of endometriosis already excised.

The surgeon identifies the upper and lower margins of the endometriotic nodule on the bowel through palpation. A straight suture is placed through these margins, preparing the nodule for insertion into the stapling device. The suture is shortened for easier management.

The surgeon advances a circular stapler into the rectum, positioning it to draw the nodule into the stapling device. The stapler is opened, the nodule is brought into the device, and then the stapler is closed, securing the nodule within.

The surgeon fires the circular stapler, ensuring the bowel is correctly positioned without mesenteric rotation. The nodule is excised, and pressure is applied posteriorly with the stapling device to excise the anterior wall of the stapling device.

After removing the stapling device, the surgeon performs a sigmoidoscopy to inspect the anastomosis site for bleeding. The anastomosis is then tested for leaks by clamping the bowel with an atraumatic grasper, filling the pelvis with fluid, and inflating the bowel with air under pressure to check for air leakage.
The surgeon removes excess fluid from the pelvic cavity, ensuring no residual fluid remains. Any additional procedures are finalized, marking the completion of the colectomy.

Multidisciplinary minimally invasive laparoscopic excision and treatment of extensive bowel endometriosis is a standard of care and recommended. Discoid resection of the invasive bowel endometriosis nodules can be safely performed depending on the size and location of the lesions.
